title: Zupa pomidorowa
subtitle: Polish tomato and rice soup
excerpt: A simple, bright tomato soup made with chicken broth, tinned tomatoes, a little cream, and short-grain or long-grain rice cooked directly in the soup. The most popular soup in the Polish home kitchen — clean, slightly sweet, and finished with a swirl of cream and fresh dill.
sourceNotes: Zupa pomidorowa (tomato soup) became a staple of the Polish table in the twentieth century, once tinned tomatoes became widely available. It is one of the most commonly eaten soups in Poland, regularly cited in surveys as the national favourite. The addition of rice cooked directly in the soup is the defining Polish feature; some families use pasta or noodles instead.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the rice and cream; both suffer on freezing. Reheat the soup, cook fresh rice separately, and add the cream when serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make the tomato-broth base in bulk and freeze; add rice and cream per serving when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The broth base keeps for 3 days in the fridge. Add rice and cream fresh when reheating.

content.text: Zupa pomidorowa is the soup that Polish children eat most and remember longest. It is simple in the way that all good soups are simple: a decent broth, good tinned tomatoes, a little cream, and rice cooked directly in the pot so it absorbs the tomato flavour as it softens. The finished soup is bright red, slightly sweet from the tomatoes, and rounded by the cream. Dill goes on top at the last moment.

The rice matters. Cook it in the soup, not separately, so the starch thickens the liquid slightly and the grains take on the tomato colour. Long-grain rice works; short-grain rice gives a slightly stodgier result that some families prefer. Do not add the rice too early or it will become mushy.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and {{carrot}} and cook for 8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft but not coloured. Add the {{tomato-puree}} and cook for 1 minute. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} and blended {{tinned-tomatoes}}. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes.

Add the {{long-grain-rice}} and simmer for 12 to 15 minutes until the rice is tender. Stir in the {{single-cream}}. Taste and season with {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{black-pepper-ground}}, and the sugar if the tomatoes are quite sharp. Serve immediately, topped with {{dill}}.

items.cause: Rice has not released enough starch, or too much stock for the tomatoes
items.fix: Simmer uncovered for 5 minutes more. The rice starch and the tomato solids will thicken it gradually

items.cause: Tinned tomatoes not cooked out fully or too little cream
items.fix: Simmer for 5 minutes more before adding the rice, then add a little more cream and a small pinch of sugar to balance the acidity

items.cause: Rice added too early or simmered too long
items.fix: Add the rice only when the broth is already fully seasoned. Watch the time: 12 to 15 minutes is usually enough

content.text: Zupa pomidorowa is regularly cited as the soup Poles know best and eat most often. It appears in school canteens, at family Sunday dinners, and as the first course of the Christmas Eve meal. Its simplicity is part of its appeal: there is nothing here that requires skill or unusual ingredients, only a decent broth and good tinned tomatoes. The rice cooked in the soup is the key detail that distinguishes the Polish version from Italian or Spanish tomato soups, which tend to be thicker and more concentrated. This one is lighter, brothier, and served hot.
